description: Refuse-or-cite Q&A GPC unit tests. Seeded from live club GPC Unit 15 Break Off & Circuit Planning Google Form.

source_forms.title: GPC Unit 15 - Break Off & Circuit Planning

items.question: The first step in finishing a soaring flight is to change from “Soaring-Pilot” to “Landing-Pilot.” What is the principal purpose of this mental switch?
items.expected_answer: Focus exclusively on the decisions required for a safe landing.
choices: Reduce cockpit noise.
Focus exclusively on the decisions required for a safe landing.
Increase thermal awareness.
Reset all instruments.

items.question: You should break-off from soaring only when you have enough height to:
items.expected_answer: Transit to the chosen circuit joining area and still monitor changing weather.
choices: Dump all water ballast.
Transit to the chosen circuit joining area and still monitor changing weather.
Cruise the airfield at 500 ft.
Perform a 360° steep turn over the field.

items.question: The Pilot Guide lists three pre-requisite GPC units for this lesson. Which unit is NOT on that list?
items.expected_answer: Unit 12 — Slow Flight & Stalling.
choices: Unit 7 — Straight Flight & Trim.
Unit 9 — Lookout Scan Procedures.
Unit 10 — Use of Ancillary Controls.
Unit 12 — Slow Flight & Stalling.

items.question: The approach speed you must determine at break-off is calculated as:
items.expected_answer: 1.5 Vs + ½ wind speed.
choices: Best-glide + 10 kt.
1.3 Vs + wind gust factor.
1.5 Vs + ½ wind speed.
VNE − 40 kt.

items.question: When selecting a landing direction, which statement is most correct?
items.expected_answer: The landing direction should normally favour into-wind landing, but slope, sun position, obstacles, traffic, and circuit considerations may also be relevant.
choices: The landing direction should always be directly into wind.
The landing direction should be chosen mainly to avoid flying a full circuit.
The landing direction should normally favour into-wind landing, but slope, sun position, obstacles, traffic, and circuit considerations may also be relevant.
The landing direction should be based on the shortest ground roll, regardless of other factors.

items.question: Which scan technique is emphasised for spotting traffic inside the circuit area before joining?
items.expected_answer: Targeted scan backed up by periodic full scans.
choices: Cruise scan only
Full scan only
Targeted scan backed up by periodic full scans.
Instrument scan.

items.question: When leaving soaring flight and transiting to join the circuit, what is the main hazard to avoid?
items.expected_answer: Joining, crossing, or descending in a way that conflicts with established circuit traffic or enters a winch-launch path.
choices: Overflying the windsock.
Joining, crossing, or descending in a way that conflicts with established circuit traffic or enters a winch-launch path.
Crossing midfield above 1,000 ft in all circumstances.
Making radio position calls.

items.question: A circuit joining area should normally be selected so that the inbound track:
items.expected_answer: Avoids unnecessary flight over the active runway or through the active circuit area before joining downwind.
choices: Avoids unnecessary flight over the active runway or through the active circuit area before joining downwind.
Takes the shortest route to the aerodrome, even if this requires crossing the active runway.
Positions the aircraft to join base leg directly, unless traffic is already established.
Allows the pilot to delay joining until late downwind to maximise soaring opportunities.

items.question: If other traffic forces a late change of plan, you must be prepared to:
items.expected_answer: Modify the circuit pattern or land off-field within the planned circuit area if necessary.
choices: Continue with the original circuit plan, provided you have made a radio call.
Modify the circuit pattern or land off-field within the planned circuit area if necessary.
Extend the circuit until the traffic conflict has passed, regardless of height remaining.
Turn away from the circuit temporarily and rejoin when the area is clear.

items.question: Below 1 000 ft AGL you must fly at or above:
items.expected_answer: 1.5 Vs (“safe speed near the ground”).
choices: Best-glide
1.3 Vs (safe manoeuvring speed).
1.5 Vs (“safe speed near the ground”).
Maximum L/D.

items.question: Which pair of complementary units does the Guide recommend you study together with Unit 15?
items.expected_answer: Unit 16 Circuit Joining & Execution and Unit 20 Launch Emergencies.
choices: Unit 16 Circuit Joining & Execution and Unit 20 Launch Emergencies.
Unit 17 Wave & Unit 30 Thermals.
Unit 18 Navigation & Unit 21 Radio.
Unit 23 Rules of the Air & Unit 24 Airmanship.

items.question: A student repeatedly selects the same circuit joining point regardless of height or wind. The probable root cause is:
items.expected_answer: Flying by rote instead of assessing current conditions.
choices: Weak radio discipline.
Flying by rote instead of assessing current conditions.
Excess ballast.
Over-use of full scan.

items.question: When height is becoming marginal, what is the most important consideration when selecting a circuit joining option?
items.expected_answer: Preserving a safe landing option while maintaining separation from other traffic.
choices: Joining on a 45° entry to downwind whenever possible.
Preserving a safe landing option while maintaining separation from other traffic.
Avoiding crosswind joins.
Completing a standard rectangular circuit regardless of height.

items.question: During circuit planning, what should a pilot do if the planned circuit may bring the aircraft close to obstacles, restricted airspace, or other hazards?
items.expected_answer: Reassess the plan early and adjust the circuit or landing option while maintaining safe separation.
choices: Reassess the plan early and adjust the circuit or landing option while maintaining safe separation.
Continue with the planned circuit unless a GPS ‘airspace’ alert is received.
Climb above normal circuit height to remain clear of all hazards.
Fly a wider circuit to create more room, regardless of height or traffic.

items.question: At break-off you set the trim to achieve the planned down-wind speed. If you later need an extra 5 kt, you should:
items.expected_answer: Lower the nose, let the glider accelerate, then re-trim.
choices: Ignore it—speed is fixed.
Lower the nose, let the glider accelerate, then re-trim.
Pull full air-brake briefly.
Raise the nose and add flap.
items.note: Inferred from Unit 7 trim practice + Unit 15 ‘Trim to an appropriate speed’.

items.question: Which statement about radio calls is correct?
items.expected_answer: At least one call when joining, plus another on base, this improves situational awareness
choices: Calls are optional in gliding circuits.
You must call on every leg.
At least one call when joining, plus another on base, this improves situational awareness
Only the duty instructor may transmit

items.question: Why is a base leg into wind generally preferred when a choice of circuit direction is available?
items.expected_answer: It provides more time for planning and reduces the likelihood of a rushed turn onto final.
choices: It ensures the shortest possible landing distance.
It provides more time for planning and reduces the likelihood of a rushed turn onto final.
It eliminates the need to adjust the circuit for wind drift.
It allows the aircraft to fly a wider circuit without losing height.

items.question: A late decision to break-off often leads to:
items.expected_answer: Rushed planning and a high-workload circuit.
choices: Extra soaring time.
Rushed planning and a high-workload circuit.
Smoother finals.
Better obstacle clearance.

items.question: During pre-landing checks you discover the undercarriage will not lock down. What is the most appropriate action?
items.expected_answer: Reassess the landing plan and, if possible, select a landing area and surface that will minimise damage if the undercarriage collapses.
choices: Continue with the planned landing, as the undercarriage may lock itself during touchdown.
Reassess the landing plan and, if possible, select a landing area and surface that will minimise damage if the undercarriage collapses.
Return to soaring flight and attempt to rectify the problem before landing.
Delay making a decision until established on final approach.
items.note: Aligned with Unit 15 reassessment / emergency options within the circuit area.

items.question: Competence for this unit requires that the glider is fully configured (flaps, gear, straps, radio, ballast, etc.) by:
items.expected_answer: Early on the down-wind leg.
choices: Mid-base leg.
Short final.
Early on the down-wind leg.
50 ft above ground.
